skills.prompt: This topic introduces natural selection for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.A, Unit 7, Topic 7.1). Students should understand Darwin's theory: natural selection acts on heritable phenotypic variation within populations. Individuals with traits better suited to their environment have higher fitness (greater reproductive success), passing advantageous alleles to offspring. Over generations, this changes allele frequencies in the population. Four conditions are required: variation exists, variation is heritable, more offspring are produced than can survive, and survival and reproduction are not random. Natural selection acts on individuals but evolution occurs in populations. Students must distinguish natural selection from other evolutionary mechanisms. Connect to evidence of evolution, adaptation, and population genetics. AP exam note: questions require applying natural selection to novel scenarios. Vocabulary: natural selection, fitness, adaptation, heritable variation, differential reproduction, population, allele frequency.

This topic covers natural selection mechanisms in depth for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.B, Unit 7, Topic 7.2). Students should understand the three modes of natural selection: directional selection (favors one extreme phenotype, shifts distribution), stabilizing selection (favors intermediate phenotype, narrows distribution), and disruptive selection (favors both extremes, widens distribution). Sexual selection is a form of natural selection driven by mate choice (intersexual selection) or competition for mates (intrasexual selection). Students must interpret graphs showing phenotype distribution before and after selection and identify the selection mode. Natural selection can only act on existing variation and does not produce perfection. Connect to population genetics, speciation, and Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium. AP exam note: graph interpretation of selection modes is commonly tested. Vocabulary: directional selection, stabilizing selection, disruptive selection, sexual selection, phenotype distribution, selective pressure.

This topic covers artificial selection for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.C, Unit 7, Topic 7.3). Students should understand that artificial selection occurs when humans selectively breed organisms for desired traits, leading to rapid phenotypic change. Examples include domesticated dogs from wolves, crop plants (corn from teosinte), and livestock breeding for milk or meat production. Artificial selection demonstrates that heritable variation exists and that selection can drive significant change, supporting Darwin's theory. However, artificial selection can reduce genetic diversity and create health problems (e.g., hip dysplasia in dogs). Students must compare artificial and natural selection, noting that both act on heritable variation but differ in the selecting agent. Connect to biotechnology, GMOs, and conservation genetics. AP exam note: questions may ask students to use artificial selection as evidence for evolution. Vocabulary: artificial selection, selective breeding, domestication, genetic diversity, trait, heritable variation.

This topic covers population genetics for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.D, Unit 7, Topic 7.4). Students should understand that evolution occurs at the population level through changes in allele frequencies. Mechanisms of evolution include natural selection, genetic drift (bottleneck effect, founder effect), gene flow (migration), and mutation. Genetic drift has a stronger effect in small populations and is random, unlike natural selection. Gene flow introduces new alleles through migration between populations, increasing variation within and decreasing variation between populations. Mutation is the ultimate source of all new alleles. Students must predict how each mechanism affects allele frequencies and genetic variation. Connect to Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, speciation, and conservation biology. AP exam note: distinguishing mechanisms of evolution is heavily tested. Vocabulary: allele frequency, genetic drift, bottleneck effect, founder effect, gene flow, mutation, population genetics, microevolution.

This topic covers Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.E, Unit 7, Topic 7.5). Students should understand that Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium describes a non-evolving population where allele and genotype frequencies remain constant across generations. Five conditions must be met: no mutation, random mating, no natural selection, extremely large population size, and no gene flow. The equations are p + q = 1 (allele frequencies) and p-squared + 2pq + q-squared = 1 (genotype frequencies), where p = dominant allele frequency and q = recessive allele frequency. Students must calculate allele and genotype frequencies from population data and determine whether a population is in equilibrium. Deviations indicate evolution is occurring. Connect to population genetics, natural selection, and genetic drift. AP exam note: Hardy-Weinberg calculations are very commonly tested. Vocabulary: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, allele frequency, genotype frequency, equilibrium conditions, evolving population.

This topic covers evidence of evolution for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.F, Unit 7, Topic 7.6). Students should understand multiple lines of evidence supporting evolution: the fossil record shows transitional forms and extinction patterns; biogeography explains species distribution based on geological history; comparative anatomy reveals homologous structures (shared ancestry) vs. analogous structures (convergent evolution) and vestigial structures; comparative embryology shows similar developmental stages among related species; molecular evidence (DNA and protein sequence comparisons) shows that closely related species have more similar sequences. Students must evaluate evidence and construct arguments for common ancestry. Connect to phylogenetics, speciation, and classification. AP exam note: questions may present molecular or anatomical data to support evolutionary relationships. Vocabulary: fossil record, homologous, analogous, vestigial, biogeography, comparative anatomy, molecular homology, convergent evolution.

This topic covers common ancestry for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.G, Unit 7, Topic 7.7). Students should understand that all life shares common ancestry, supported by universal features: DNA as genetic material, the universal genetic code, shared metabolic pathways (glycolysis), conserved genes (e.g., Hox genes), and similar cellular structures. Molecular comparisons provide quantitative evidence: species with more similar DNA or protein sequences diverged more recently. Phylogenetic trees depict evolutionary relationships based on shared derived characteristics. Molecular clocks use mutation rates to estimate divergence times. Students must use molecular data to support claims of common ancestry and construct or interpret phylogenetic trees. Connect to classification, speciation, and biodiversity. AP exam note: cladogram interpretation is frequently tested. Vocabulary: common ancestor, phylogenetic tree, cladogram, molecular clock, conserved sequence, homology, divergence, universal genetic code.

This topic covers continuing evolution for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.H, Unit 7, Topic 7.8). Students should understand that evolution is an ongoing process observable in real time. Examples include antibiotic resistance in bacteria (natural selection favoring resistant mutants), pesticide resistance in insects, evolution of beak size in Galapagos finches in response to drought, and the rise of drug-resistant HIV. These examples demonstrate that environmental changes create new selective pressures, driving rapid evolution in populations with short generation times. Students must explain how human activities accelerate evolution in other species and why resistance evolves. Connect to natural selection, mutation, population genetics, and public health. AP exam note: questions may present data on resistance evolution and ask for explanations using evolutionary principles. Vocabulary: antibiotic resistance, selective pressure, adaptation, microevolution, generation time, resistant allele, overuse of antibiotics.

This topic covers phylogeny for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.I, Unit 7, Topic 7.9). Students should understand that phylogenetic trees (cladograms) represent hypotheses about evolutionary relationships based on shared derived characteristics (synapomorphies). Cladograms show branching patterns: nodes represent common ancestors, branch tips represent taxa, and outgroups root the tree. Phylogenies can be constructed using morphological data, molecular sequences, or both. Maximum parsimony and molecular clock analyses are used to build and evaluate trees. Students must interpret cladograms to determine relationships, identify common ancestors, and infer traits of ancestral species. Connect to classification, evidence of evolution, and molecular biology. AP exam note: cladogram reading and construction are core skills. Vocabulary: phylogeny, cladogram, node, branch, outgroup, synapomorphy, monophyletic, parsimony, taxon, derived character.

This topic covers speciation for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.J, Unit 7, Topic 7.10). Students should understand that speciation is the formation of new species when populations become reproductively isolated. Allopatric speciation occurs when geographic barriers physically separate populations (e.g., mountain ranges, rivers), and genetic divergence accumulates over time. Sympatric speciation occurs without geographic separation, often through polyploidy (especially in plants) or habitat differentiation. Reproductive isolation mechanisms include prezygotic barriers (temporal, behavioral, mechanical, gametic isolation) and postzygotic barriers (hybrid inviability, sterility, breakdown). Students must distinguish speciation types and identify isolation mechanisms. Connect to natural selection, genetic drift, and biodiversity. AP exam note: scenarios requiring identification of speciation type are common. Vocabulary: speciation, allopatric, sympatric, reproductive isolation, prezygotic, postzygotic, polyploidy, geographic barrier.

This topic covers extinction for AP Biology students (CED: EVO-7.K, Unit 7, Topic 7.11). Students should understand that extinction occurs when a species can no longer survive and reproduce in its environment. Causes include environmental change, competition, disease, predation, and catastrophic events. Mass extinctions have occurred five times in Earth's history, each followed by adaptive radiation as surviving lineages diversify into newly available niches. The current biodiversity crisis is driven by human activities: habitat destruction, overexploitation, pollution, invasive species, and climate change. Background extinction is the normal rate of species loss between mass extinction events. Students must explain how extinction affects biodiversity and ecosystem function. Connect to natural selection, ecological succession, and conservation biology. AP exam note: questions may link extinction to adaptive radiation or human impact. Vocabulary: extinction, mass extinction, adaptive radiation, background extinction, biodiversity, niche, endangered species.

This topic covers variations in populations for AP Biology students (CED: SYI-7.A, Unit 7, Topic 7.12). Students should understand that genetic variation within populations is maintained by several mechanisms: mutation introduces new alleles, sexual reproduction (meiosis, crossing over, random fertilization) shuffles existing alleles, and balancing selection (heterozygote advantage, frequency-dependent selection) preserves multiple alleles in a population. High genetic variation increases a population's ability to adapt to changing environments. Low variation (due to genetic drift, bottleneck, or inbreeding) reduces adaptive potential and increases extinction risk. Students must explain how variation is generated and maintained. Connect to Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, natural selection, and conservation genetics. AP exam note: questions may ask why maintaining genetic diversity matters. Vocabulary: genetic variation, heterozygote advantage, frequency-dependent selection, balancing selection, inbreeding, bottleneck, gene pool.

skills.skill: SYI Origin of Life on Earth
skills.prompt: This topic covers the origin of life on Earth for AP Biology students (CED: SYI-7.B, Unit 7, Topic 7.13). Students should understand scientific hypotheses about life's origins: early Earth's atmosphere lacked O2 and contained reducing gases. The Miller-Urey experiment demonstrated that organic molecules (amino acids) could form abiotically under early Earth conditions. The RNA world hypothesis proposes that self-replicating RNA preceded DNA and proteins, as RNA can both store information and catalyze reactions (ribozymes). Protocells (lipid vesicles enclosing organic molecules) may have preceded true cells. The earliest fossil evidence of life dates to about 3.5 billion years ago (stromatolites). Students must evaluate evidence for each hypothesis. Connect to evolution, biochemistry, and astrobiology. AP exam note: questions may ask students to evaluate experimental evidence for abiogenesis. Vocabulary: abiogenesis, Miller-Urey experiment, RNA world, ribozyme, protocell, stromatolite, reducing atmosphere, organic molecule.
